stem: How many functions from a set of [[m]] distinct elements onto a set of [[k]] distinct elements are there? (That is, how many surjections map the [[m]]-element domain onto every one of the [[k]] elements in the codomain?)
solution: By inclusion-exclusion the number of surjections is \( \sum_{i=0}^{[[k]]} (-1)^i \binom{[[k]]}{i} ([[k]]-i)^{[[m]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts ALL functions \( [[k]]^{[[m]]} \), including those that miss some elements.
This subtracts only one class of non-surjective functions, not all of them.
This chooses which [[k]] domain elements hit the codomain and permutes, over/undercounting.
This counts injections \( {}_{[[m]]}P_{[[k]]} \) rather than surjections.
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].

stem: How many functions from a set of [[m]] distinct elements onto a 2-element set \( \{A, B\} \) are there? (Every function must use both A and B somewhere in its range.)
solution: There are \( 2^{[[m]]} \) functions in total; exactly 2 of them are constant (all-A or all-B) and so fail to be onto. Removing those gives \( 2^{[[m]]} - 2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts ALL functions to \( \{A,B\} \), including the two constant ones.
This removes only one of the two constant functions.
This halves the total instead of removing the two constant functions.
This confuses the function count with a permutation count.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.

stem: How many functions from a set of [[m]] distinct elements to a set of [[k]] distinct elements FAIL to be onto (that is, miss at least one of the [[k]] elements in the codomain)?
solution: The number of onto functions is \( \sum_{i=0}^{[[k]]} (-1)^i \binom{[[k]]}{i} ([[k]]-i)^{[[m]]} \); subtracting this from the \( [[k]]^{[[m]]} \) total functions leaves \( [[answer]] \) functions that are NOT onto.
distractor_rationales: This is the total number of functions, forgetting to remove the onto ones.
This is an unrelated count of injections times a binomial factor.
This subtracts only \( [[k]]! \), not the full onto count, from the total.
This uses only the first inclusion-exclusion term instead of the full alternating sum.
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].

stem: [[m]] distinct gifts are to be distributed among [[k]] distinct children so that every child receives at least one gift. In how many ways can the gifts be distributed?
solution: This is the number of onto functions from the [[m]] gifts to the [[k]] children, given by inclusion-exclusion: \( \sum_{i=0}^{[[k]]} (-1)^i \binom{[[k]]}{i} ([[k]]-i)^{[[m]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This allows some children to receive nothing, counting ALL distributions.
This counts ordered selections of [[k]] gifts rather than distributing all [[m]] gifts.
This chooses which gifts form each child's share without correctly avoiding empty-handed children.
This subtracts only one class of invalid distributions, not all of them.
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].
This is off from the correct count by [[k]].
